sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 66, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 65 actions; a strong score needs about 54 or fewer. You are 1.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! When I pressed DOWN, B[49] changed from b4edb3faf810133b to a2774f59f95f9997. So DOWN cycles in the opposite direction from UP. Let me verify:
- UP: cycles forward through the cycle
- DOWN: cycles backward through the cycle
Also, the cursor moved from id=62 to id=61 (both are white cursor blocks). The cursor seems to be on the bottom row (row with P tiles).
Now B[49] = a2774f59f95f9997, which doesn't match any top tile. I need to get back to a matching state.
But more importantly, I still don't understand the goal. Let me reconsider.
Current state:
- B[43] (S, col 1): matches top 30
- B[49] (P, col 0): a2774f59f95f9997 (no match)
- B[50] (P, col 1): fb80c9b281b063da (matches top 18)
- B[51] (P, col 2): 5fcaa599a8d90976 (no match, can't change)
- B[52] (P, col 3): f4469c244897540c (matches top 20)
- B[53] (P, col 4): cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 (matches top 6)
Wait - maybe the goal is about the ORDER of the symbols in the bottom row matching a specific sequence from the top. Let me think about this differently.
Top P tiles in reading order: 6, 8, 18, 20, 31, 33
Their hashes: cddd7d2c6a6ceea0, d38ad0a046a963a0, fb80c9b281b063da, f4469c244897540c, 12ee96cc93cd3e0a, b4edb3faf810133b
Bottom P tiles: B[49], B[50], B[51], B[52], B[53]
Currently: a2774f59f95f9997, fb80c9b281b063da, 5fcaa599a8d90976, f4469c244897540c, cddd7d2c6a6ceea0
Hmm, let me look at the bottom P row as a sequence and see if it matches a subsequence of the top P tiles:
- B[53] = cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 = top 6 (first top P)
- B[50] = fb80c9b281b063da = top 18 (third top P)
- B[52] = f4469c244897540c = top 20 (fourth top P)
If the bottom row should read as the top P tiles in order (6, 8, 18, 20, 31):
- B[49] should be top 6: cddd7d2c6a6ceea0
- B[50] should be top 8: d38ad0a046a963a0
- B[51] should be top 18: fb80c9b281b063da
- B[52] should be top 20: f4469c244897540c ✓
- B[53] should be top 31: 12ee96cc93cd3e0a
But B[51] and B[53] can't be changed, so this doesn't work.
What about the reverse order (33, 31, 20, 18, 8)?
- B[49] = top 33: b4edb3faf810133b
- B[50] = top 31: 12ee96cc93cd3e0a
- B[51] = top 20: f4469c244897540c (but B[51] can't change and is 5fcaa599a8d90976)
Nope.
What about matching by column? Top col 1 has P tiles 6, 18, 31. If bottom col 1 (B[50]) should match one of these... B[50] = fb80c9b281b063da = top 18. That works!
Top col 3 has P tiles 8, 20, 33. Bottom col 3 (B[52]) = f4469c244897540c = top 20. That works!
Top col 1 has P tiles 6, 18, 31. Bottom col 4 (B[53]) = cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 = top 6. Hmm, col 4 doesn't correspond to any top column.
Wait, let me think about the layout again. The top grid is 3 rows × 4 cols. The bottom is 2 rows × 5 cols. Maybe the bottom columns don't directly correspond to top columns.
Let me try yet another hypothesis: maybe the bottom P row should match the SECOND ROW of the top grid (row 1): S[17], P[18], S[19], P[20]. The P tiles in row 1 are 18 and 20.
- B[50] = top 18 ✓
- B[52] = top 20 ✓
But what about B[49], B[51], B[53]?
Or maybe the bottom P row should match ALL P tiles from the top, but only the ones that can be changed need to be set correctly, and the fixed ones are already correct?
Fixed P tiles: B[51] (5fcaa599a8d90976 - no match), B[52] (f4469c244897540c = top 20), B[53] (cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 = top 6)
Changeable P tiles: B[49], B[50]
If B[51] can't match any top tile, then the goal can't be to match all bottom P tiles to top P tiles.
I'm really stuck. Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is to make the bottom row display a specific message or pattern, and the symbols have specific meanings. Or maybe the goal is about the number of matching tiles reaching a threshold.
Actually, let me reconsider the initial state. In the initial state:
- B[53] = cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 (matches top 6)
- B[43] = 598e9cbf8e60024f (matches top 30)
All other bottom tiles didn't match. Maybe the goal is to maximize the number of matches, and the current state (with 4-5 matches) is already close to optimal?
But the level isn't complete, so there must be more to do.
Let me try a different hypothesis: maybe I need to match the bottom S row to the top S tiles as well. The S tiles can't be changed, but maybe some of them already match and I need to check if all matchable ones are matched.
Top S tiles: 5, 7, 17, 19, 30, 32
Their hashes: 5ff2569c3387e9ef, eedf8832739058cd, d868ee08e5af84ee, e668cf5bf91b30aa, 598e9cbf8e60024f, 88e45be75860ff8c
Bottom S tiles:
- B[42]: a5a03333dcefae48 - no match
- B[43]: 598e9cbf8e60024f - matches top 30 ✓
- B[44]: 9fd5f2ec9386bc10 - no match
- B[45]: bb9796c6c98e1001 - no match
- B[46]: 8e4cb671a12a4302 - no match
Only B[43] matches. The S tiles can't be changed, so this is the maximum for S tiles.
OK let me try yet another approach. Maybe the goal is about the cursor position. Perhaps I need to navigate the cursor to a specific position to "submit" or "complete" the level. Let me look at the cursor positions more carefully.
Looking at the image, there are white blocks below the bottom P row. These might be cursor positions. Let me check if moving the cursor to a specific position triggers level completion.
Actually, I just noticed something. In the image, there are TWO white cursor blocks - one above the P row (below the S row) and one below the P row. Maybe these represent the current column selection, and the cursor can be in different "modes" or positions.
Let me try moving the cursor around to see if certain positions trigger completion.
Actually, let me first restore B[49] to a matching state since I accidentally broke it with the DOWN press. Then I'll experiment with cursor positions.
B[49] is currently a2774f59f95f9997. The cycle for col 0 P is:
6610c6e38ccbd08b → 8b72057039a2be38 → f4469c244897540c → 04a8e75f48884ca4 → a2774f59f95f9997 → b4edb3faf810133b → cddd7d2c6a6ceea0
Current: a2774f59f95f9997 (position 4)
To get to b4edb3faf810133b (position 5, matches top 33): 1 UP
To get to cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 (position 6, matches top 2): 2 UPs
To get to f4469c244897540c (position 2, matches top 16): need to wrap around, 4 UPs or 3 DOWNs
Let me try pressing UP once to get to b4edb3faf810133b (top 33), which gives unique matches.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# DOWN cycles backward, UP cycles forward
# B[49] currently a2774f59f95f9997, need to get to a matching value
# Let me restore it to b4edb3faf810133b (top 33) with 1 UP
# Then try moving cursor to different positions to see if level completes

action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'B' and n['id'] == 49:
        print(f"B[49]: {n['hash']}")

# Now let me try pressing RIGHT to move cursor and see if that triggers anything
# First check cursor positions
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W':
        print(f"Cursor: id={n['id']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Let me try a sequence: move cursor to each position and check
# Current cursor is at col 0 (from the LEFT action earlier)
# Let me try moving RIGHT through all columns

# First, let me check if the level completes when all P tiles have unique matches
# Current matches: B[43]->30, B[49]->33, B[50]->18, B[52]->20, B[53]->6
# All unique! But level didn't complete before...

# Maybe I need to try a completely different approach
# Let me check if there are any other interactive elements

# Let me look at the ascii to understand the layout better
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"\nGrid has {len(lines)} rows")
for i, line in enumerate(lines):
    if line.strip():
        print(f"Row {i}: len={len(line)}, first 80 chars: {line[:80]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

All other bottom tiles didn't match. Maybe the goal is to maximize the number of matches, and the current state (with 4-5 matches) is already close to optimal?
But the level isn't complete, so there must be more to do.
Let me try a different hypothesis: maybe I need to match the bottom S row to the top S tiles as well. The S tiles can't be changed, but maybe some of them already match and I need to check if all matchable ones are matched.

OK let me try yet another approach. Maybe the goal is about the cursor position. Perhaps I need to navigate the cursor to a specific position to "submit" or "complete" the level. Let me look at the cursor positions more carefully.
Looking at the image, there are white blocks below the bottom P row. These might be cursor positions. Let me check if moving the cursor to a specific position triggers level completion.
Actually, I just noticed something. In the image, there are TWO white cursor blocks - one above the P row (below the S row) and one below the P row. Maybe these represent the current column selection, and the cursor can be in different "modes" or positions.
Let me try moving the cursor around to see if certain positions trigger completion.
Actually, let me first restore B[49] to a matching state since I accidentally broke it with the DOWN press. Then I'll experiment with cursor positions.

To get to cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 (position 6, matches top 2): 2 UPs
To get to f4469c244897540c (position 2, matches top 16): need to wrap around, 4 UPs or 3 DOWNs

# DOWN cycles backward, UP cycles forward

# Then try moving cursor to different positions to see if level completes

action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'B' and n['id'] == 49:
        print(f"B[49]: {n['hash']}")

# Now let me try pressing RIGHT to move cursor and see if that triggers anything

for n in seg['nodes']:

# Maybe I need to try a completely different approach

# Let me look at the ascii to understand the layout better
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"\nGrid has {len(lines)} rows")
for i, line in enumerate(lines):
    if line.strip():
        print(f"Row {i}: len={len(line)}, first 80 chars: {line[:80]}")
</parameter>
</function>
